topic: weekend lake trip with the boys

turns.content: dude so me and the boys are tryna plan a lake trip for presidents day weekend and I need help figurin out what we need to bring cuz last time we went campin we forgot like half the stuff

Ha, forgetting half the stuff is a rite of passage for camping trips honestly. Let's make sure you're covered this time. First off — where are you going, and are you tent camping or is there a cabin involved? And how many guys are we talking?

theres a spot up in new hampshire my buddy knows about near lake winnepesaukee and were gonna rent one of those little cabins so at least we got a roof and a bathroom lol its gonna be like 5 or 6 of us

Okay perfect, a cabin near Winnipesaukee makes things way easier than tent camping in February. That said, it's still going to be cold up there — probably in the 20s or teens at night. Here's what I'd focus on:

For the cabin essentials, check what's included in the rental. Some come with basic cookware and linens, some don't. You'll want to confirm that before you pack. For food and drinks, plan meals ahead so one person isn't stuck doing all of it. And for gear — layers, layers, layers. Even if you're mostly hanging inside, you'll want warm stuff for being outside by the fire or whatever.

Want me to break it down into an actual packing list?
yeah bro do the list thing thats exactly what I need cuz if I dont write it down someone is gonna forget the food or somethin stupid like that

Here's a solid breakdown for 5-6 guys, a cabin, and a February weekend:

**Food & Drinks:**
- Breakfast stuff: eggs, bacon, bread, butter, coffee
- Lunch/snacks: sandwich supplies, chips, beef jerky, trail mix
- Dinner: burgers or steaks for the grill, hot dogs as backup, baked beans, rolls
- Drinks: whatever you guys are drinking, plus water and Gatorade
- Condiments and cooking oil

**Cooking & Kitchen:**
- Coolers with plenty of ice (2 minimum — one for food, one for drinks)
- Paper plates, cups, plastic utensils
- Trash bags
- Lighter and fire starters
- Charcoal if there's a grill, or firewood for a firepit

**Clothing & Gear:**
- Thermal base layers, heavy jacket, hat, gloves
- Extra socks and a dry change of clothes
- Boots
- Sleeping bag or extra blankets in case the cabin's heating is weak

**Other:**
- Bluetooth speaker
- Cards, games
- First aid kit
- Flashlights or headlamps
- Phone chargers and a power strip

Split it up between everyone so no one person is hauling everything. Want me to help divide it up?
bro thats clutch. yeah we should split it up. so theres me and danny and sully and mike and probably kevin if he can get off work. I feel like I should handle the drinks and maybe the grill stuff

Good call taking drinks and grill since you're already thinking about it. Here's a rough split:

- **You**: All drinks, cooler for drinks, grill stuff (charcoal, lighter, tongs, burgers/steaks/dogs)
- **Danny**: Breakfast supplies (eggs, bacon, bread, coffee, butter), cooking oil, paper plates and utensils
- **Sully**: Lunch and snack stuff (sandwich supplies, chips, jerky, trail mix), trash bags, condiments
- **Mike**: Firewood, fire starters, first aid kit, flashlights, power strip
- **Kevin**: Food cooler with ice, extra blankets or sleeping bags, speaker, cards and games

Kevin's stuff is easy to redistribute if he can't make it. Just send this to the group chat and have everyone confirm so nobody shows up empty-handed.
this is perfect dude im screenshottin this and sendin it right now. oh also do you think the lake is gonna be frozen enough to walk on or is that sketchy in february

Winnipesaukee usually has solid ice by mid-February, and people do go ice fishing out there pretty regularly. That said, I'd be careful — ice thickness can vary a lot depending on the spot, recent weather, and whether there's been any warm spells. General rule is you want at least 4 inches of clear ice to walk on safely.

If you see other people out on it or there's an ice fishing community around, that's usually a good sign. But I wouldn't just wander out on your own without checking conditions. You could call the cabin rental place or a local bait shop and ask what the ice is looking like that week.
good call ill ask when we book the cabin. sully keeps sayin we should bring ice fishin stuff but literally none of us have ever ice fished in our lives lol

Ha, that's honestly how most people get into ice fishing — someone in the group says "we should do it" and everyone just figures it out on the fly. You can actually rent augers and tip-ups from bait shops up there, so you don't need to buy a bunch of equipment for a first time. It's pretty simple once you get set up — drill holes, drop lines, and sit around talking while you wait. Basically just hanging out in the cold with the boys. Could be a fun addition to the trip even if you don't catch anything.
that actually sounds sick honestly just sittin out on the ice with some drinks and the boys. do we need like a license or somethin for that or can you just show up and fish

Yeah you'll need a New Hampshire freshwater fishing license. You can buy one online through the state's Fish and Game website — it takes like five minutes and you can get a short-term one for just a few days, which is probably your best bet since you're only there for a weekend. Everyone who's fishing needs their own license though, so make sure the whole crew gets one if you're all gonna drop lines.

alright im sold bro we're doin it. worst case we freeze our butts off and catch nothin and its still a good story. thanks for the help with all this dude I woulda showed up with just a bag of chips and a speaker

Haha, chips and a speaker is a vibe but it's not gonna keep 5 guys fed for a weekend. You're all set now though — just send that list out, book the cabin, and you guys are going to have a great time. Winnipesaukee in winter is beautiful. Have fun out there!
